title: The Prouerbis of Wysdom (Rawlinson poet. 32 compilation)
summary: CME00074 is the repository copy of a University of Michigan electronic transcription of Julius Zupitza's 1893 article The Prouerbis of Wysdom in Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Litteraturen 90, pp. 241–268. The article directly transcribes Bodleian Library MS. Rawl. poet. 32, fols. 54v–55r: a 146-line proverb compilation, a separately rubricated ten-line Ten Commandments text, and twelve appended moral lines. Zupitza's German introduction and extensive notes are also encoded, so the digital item is broader than the medieval compilation. A complete 500-page scan of the journal volume is cached and maps the article to PDF pages 255–282. The official Digital Bodleian object publishes only two selected manuscript images, fols. 30r and 57r; the cache contains that complete two-canvas published selection, but it has no overlap with the target fols. 54v–55r and remains partial manuscript coverage. No complete public digital facsimile of the manuscript was found. The whole-object request route therefore asks for the complete current physical manuscript—including covers, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, every leaf, and edges where offered—and maps the target work separately to fols. 54v–55r.
review_notes: The exact cataloged medieval subject is the 168-line Rawlinson compilation. Zupitza's German introduction and notes are encoded editorial matter, not intellectual components of the medieval work.

Zupitza says the work is probably not unified: he treats lines 129–136 as a later addition, probably connects lines 137–146 with that addition, identifies lines 147–156 as a still younger Ten Commandments section, and describes lines 157–168 as appended teachings. Those are editorial arguments rather than separately dated physical strata established by the manuscript catalog.

DIMEV describes Prouerbis of Wysdom as 106 lines in couplets, while the Rawlinson witness's main rubricated component contains 146 lines before the Ten Commandments. The discrepancy reflects the fluid, manuscript-specific proverb tradition and remains explicit.

Edden and Thompson recommend treating each proverb compilation as a separate item and the organizing role as compilation rather than assuming a single stable authorial work. Their witness list says Rawlinson poet. 33, an evident shelfmark typo for poet. 32.
The Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries record cites Zupitza 1867 for the target item; Zupitza's scanned article and journal title page establish the relevant publication as 1893.

The complete Digital Bodleian published inventory was visually audited: image 1 is fol. 30r and image 2 is fol. 57r, at 1800 × 2734 and 1800 × 2751 pixels. Neither image contains the work on fols. 54v–55r.

Digital Bodleian metadata names Sulpicius as author and John Lydgate as translator for other contents of the composite manuscript. Those inherited labels are not responsibilities for this proverb compilation.

Whole-object completeness is evaluated against the physical manuscript, not the target work. A future supplied facsimile should be cached as a complete manuscript object and then given a separate work_portion for fols. 54v–55r.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00074
entities.description: A public digital transcription of Zupitza's complete article, preserving the German introduction and notes, the 168-line Middle English text, printed page breaks, manuscript folio milestones, line numbering, and italicized expansions or supplied letters.

entities.label: Julius Zupitza, The Prouerbis of Wysdom (1893)
entities.description: Zupitza's first known printing of the Rawlinson compilation, with a German introduction, transcription on pp. 243–248, and comparative proverb notes on pp. 249–268.

bibliographic.citation: Julius Zupitza, 'The Prouerbis of Wysdom,' Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Litteraturen 90, 47th year (Braunschweig: George Westermann, 1893), 241–268.
bibliographic.title: The Prouerbis of Wysdom
bibliographic.container_title: Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Litteraturen

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Rawl. poet. 32
alternate_labels: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Rawlinson poet. 32
entities.description: A composite English and Latin anthology assembled in six booklets in the second half of the fifteenth century. The target compilation occupies fols. 54v–55r as manuscript items 8 and 9; several scribes write fols. 54v–168r in Secretary script, and fols. 54v–57r use red brackets and red initial letters.

date_statements.label: Manuscript production
date_statements.value: Second half of the fifteenth century (1450–1500)
date_statements.notes: This dates the physical witness, not every proverb's composition.

physical_description.extent: ii modern paper flyleaves, ii parchment leaves foliated 1–2, and 206 folios; fols. 205–206 are flyleaves and fol. 206 is modern paper
physical_description.dimensions: Leaves approximately 280 × 195 mm; binding approximately 280 × 200 mm
physical_description.format: Composite codex anthology in six booklets
notes: The current non-medieval binding is white leather over pasteboard with four barely raised spine bands.
The catalog gives the manuscript's origin as unknown and identifies the scribal language of the relevant section broadly with western Leicestershire or Warwickshire.
A leaf is lost after fol. 53 and fol. 54r is blank; the target starts on fol. 54v.
Richard Turnowre wrote his name on fol. 205r around 1500.

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of Archiv volume 90 (1893)
entities.description: A 500-page complete-volume PDF. Zupitza's article is printed on pp. 241–268 and appears on PDF pages 255–282 / Internet Archive leaves n255–n282.

entities.label: Digital Bodleian selected images of MS. Rawl. poet. 32
entities.description: The provider's entire published object consists of two canvases, fol. 30r and fol. 57r. It is complete only as a cache of that selected digital object and partial relative to the manuscript; it contains no target-work image.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation 2 manifest with two color images

entities.label: Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries record for Rawl. poet. 32
entities.description: The detailed scholarly manuscript record used for current shelfmark, contents, target loci, date, language, hands, decoration, collation, binding, foliation, origin, and provenance.

entities.label: DIMEV records 5530 and 323
entities.description: Versioned scholarly index records for Prouerbis of Wysdom (IMEV 3502) and the Ten Commandments text (IMEV 176), including witness loci and edition references.

entities.label: Edden and Thompson, Middle English Verse Proverbs (2000)
entities.description: A peer-reviewed study and descriptive listing that treats proverb compilations as fluid manuscript-specific versions and discusses IMEV 3502.

bibliographic.citation: Valerie J. Edden and Caroline Thompson, 'Middle English Verse Proverbs: The Problem of Classification,' Leeds Studies in English, new series 31 (2000): 173–203.
bibliographic.title: Middle English Verse Proverbs: The Problem of Classification

agents.name: Anonymous scribes of Rawlinson poet. 32
notes: A modeling convenience for an unnamed group, not a historical institution. The MWM record says several scribes wrote fols. 54v–168r in Secretary script; it does not isolate one named target-work scribe.

agents.name: Digital Index of Middle English Verse

object.description: Zupitza's entire article, including German introduction, Middle English transcription, and German notes.
The manuscript compilation on the target leaves.

assertion.notes: Zupitza explicitly identifies Rawlinson poet. 32 and supplies manuscript folio and reading notes throughout the direct transcription.

subject.description: The article within the complete journal scan.

scope.notes: The cached source retains the complete journal volume.

subject.description: The entire two-canvas published Digital Bodleian selection.

object.description: The complete manuscript, including target items 8 and 9.
Prouerbis of Wysdom and Ten Commandments components in Rawlinson poet. 32.
The manuscript-specific Prouerbis of Wysdom version and its relation to the wider proverb tradition.

assertion.notes: The article's Rawlinson poet. 33 is treated as a typographical error because its locus, edition, and context identify Rawlinson poet. 32.

access.access_method: Open the stable U-M name handle or find the title under Anonymous/Proverbs in the CME browse list.

work_portion.label: Zupitza, The Prouerbis of Wysdom
notes: The complete volume is retained; the work mapping does not crop the article from its journal context.

access.access_method: Open the Digital Bodleian object or IIIF manifest. The complete published inventory has two images only.

local_copies.label: Complete published two-image Digital Bodleian selection for MS Rawl. poet. 32
notes: ZIP contains the exact provider manifest, inventory, and every one of its two published canvas images.
The selected folios are 30r and 57r. Neither overlaps the target work at fols. 54v–55r, so no work_portion is asserted.
Both images use exact width-only IIIF requests with the 1800, size segment; ZIP integrity, hashes, labels, dimensions, and visual folio identities were audited.
Coverage is partial relative to the physical manuscript even though every image in this selected provider object has been cached.
Do not present the object as a digitized manuscript facsimile of the target work.

access.access_method: Use the selected Digital Bodleian object for discovery, then request a complete reproduction from Bodleian Imaging Services.
notes: Dated negative scope: official Medieval Bodleian catalog, Digital Bodleian object and search, IIIF manifest inventory, and exact-shelfmark facsimile searches on 2026-07-12.
The two-image public object is not evidence that the target leaves or complete codex have been digitized.

access.access_method: Ask Imaging Services for a complete color digital reproduction of the current physical object, not a cropped work extract. Specify covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, every leaf, edges and detached matter where offered; separately identify the work at fols. 54v–55r.
access.format: Repository-supplied digital images or reference PDF, subject to quotation

local_copies.label: Bodleian Imaging Services image order form
notes: Supporting request document only; this is not a manuscript facsimile.

local_copies.label: Bodleian Imaging Services terms of use
notes: Supporting rights document only; this is not a manuscript facsimile.

Current Special Collections mediated-copying guidance states £1 per scan for non-Oxford users for reference PDF scans, subject to condition, handling, format, extent, and Imaging Services quotation; a complete color facsimile may follow a different quoted service.
A supplied complete facsimile belongs in the ignored private cache until its exact delivery terms and publication permission are recorded.
If the repository cannot reproduce the complete object, request a written list of omitted physical components and then preserve the delivery as partial coverage.

access.access_method: Register as a Bodleian reader and order the manuscript for consultation at the Weston Library under current Special Collections procedures.

rights.component: Electronic text as distributed by the University of Michigan CME collection
rights.basis: The current U-M CME collection page states that the public-domain source editions and collection materials are public domain in the United States and applies No Copyright–United States.
notes: This assertion is attached to the U-M distribution and does not determine Bodleian manuscript-image rights.

rights.basis: The article was published in 1893 and is in the public domain in the United States.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan files and access layer
rights.basis: The underlying 1893 publication is public domain in the United States, but no item-specific open license for the scan files was captured from the Internet Archive metadata used here.
The anonymous Middle English content and fifteenth-century manuscript are centuries outside copyright term; this does not grant rights in modern photographs.

rights.component: The two Digital Bodleian manuscript photographs
rights.jurisdiction: Provider-stated terms; worldwide reuse subject to applicable law
rights.basis: The item attribution states Photo © Bodleian Libraries and CC BY-NC 4.0; Digital Bodleian terms require attribution and limit licensed reuse to noncommercial purposes.
notes: The current Git-ignored research cache does not itself authorize publication in a commercial or profit-enabled print-on-demand repository workflow.
The selected images do not contain the target work.

rights.component: Any manuscript images supplied through Imaging Services
rights.basis: Bodleian Imaging Services distinguishes noncommercial or academic uses from commercial and nonacademic publication, which requires separate permission; the exact delivery terms control any supplied files.
notes: Request explicit written permission before committing supplied images to Git, publishing them in the viewer, redistributing them, or using them in commercial print-on-demand products.
Private visual research into abbreviation marks, layout, rubrication, and editorial expansions is distinct from reproducing the images.

rights.component: Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries XML catalogue release
rights.basis: The Oxford Text Archive item metadata licenses the catalogue release CC BY-NC-SA 3.0.
The versioned Zenodo release and included license identify CC BY 4.0.
The 2000 article remains within ordinary copyright term; public download did not establish an open-content license.

editorial_practices.description: Zupitza says he regularized the use of capital letters.
editorial_practices.representation: Normalized print capitalization rather than diplomatic manuscript capitalization

editorial_practices.description: Zupitza says he supplied punctuation and occasionally hyphens.
Italic type marks expanded abbreviations and supplied letters in Zupitza's transcription; it is not ordinary rhetorical emphasis.
editorial_practices.source_feature: Manuscript abbreviations, omitted letters, and uncertain readings
editorial_practices.representation: Italic print segments retained as HI REND=italic in CME00074
notes: A complete target-folio facsimile is still needed to compare every italic expansion with the manuscript's actual marks.

editorial_practices.description: Zupitza describes the opening title, Ten Commandments heading, and closing rubric in notes rather than reproducing their red color typographically; CME retains those descriptions as notes.
editorial_practices.source_feature: Red rubrication and red underlining or initial treatment
editorial_practices.representation: Black print plus German editorial notes

editorial_practices.description: The encoding selects Zupitza's complete article from the larger Archiv volume and includes its preface, text, and notes.
editorial_practices.representation: Three DIV1 blocks: preface, poem, and notes

evidence.summary: Identifies CME00074 and preserves the entire encoded Zupitza article: German preface, 168-line Middle English compilation, notes, printed page breaks, manuscript folio milestones, rubric descriptions, and italic expansions or supplies.

evidence.locator: ETS/TEXT element counts and division boundaries
evidence.summary: Counts 3 DIV1, 1 DIV2, 3 LG, 168 L, 95 P, 29 NOTE, 28 PB, and 37 MILESTONE elements; confirms poem lines 1–146, Ten Commandments lines 147–156, and appended teachings lines 157–168.

evidence.citation: Julius Zupitza, 'The Prouerbis of Wysdom,' Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Litteraturen 90 (1893): 241–268.
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for bibliographic identity, direct use of Rawlinson poet. 32, fols. 54v–55r, the 168-line transcription, Zupitza's internal-strata argument, tentative Eastern Midlands localization, editorial capitalization and punctuation, italic expansions, rubric descriptions, and notes.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive, Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Litteraturen, volume 90.
evidence.summary: Supports public access to the complete scanned volume and exact direct PDF URL; internal title page, not generic repository date metadata, establishes 1893.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse.
evidence.summary: Describes manual keying from public-domain editions, public access, and the collection's No Copyright–United States rights statement.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, CME browse list, Anonymous/Proverbs entry for The prouerbis of wysdom.

evidence.summary: Official stable catalog route for the current shelfmark and manuscript description; automated retrieval was challenge-protected, so substantive fields were checked against the downloadable MWM source record.

evidence.citation: Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, Oxford, Bodleian Library, Rawl. poet. 32, in Oxford Text Archive item 2508.
evidence.summary: Supports the 1450–1500 date, English and Latin contents, western Leicestershire or Warwickshire scribal-language statement, target items and loci, rubric and incipit/explicit, composite-booklet structure, material, collation, hands, decoration, binding, foliation, unknown origin, and provenance.
notes: The supplied TEI has malformed spacing in two xref attributes but the official ZIP is preserved byte-for-byte.
Its Zupitza 1867 citation conflicts with the verified 1893 article and is retained as a catalog error rather than normalized silently.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian, MS. Rawl. poet. 32, object 2b3d3041-515d-4690-83ba-476c32da9671.
evidence.locator: Object metadata, image count, date, material, attribution
evidence.summary: Identifies the shelfmark and a two-image partial digital object, dates the manuscript to the second half of the fifteenth century, labels England and paper at the object level, and states Photo © Bodleian Libraries, CC BY-NC 4.0.
notes: The page's inherited Sulpicius and John Lydgate responsibility fields describe other manuscript contents, not the target work.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries IIIF Presentation manifest for MS. Rawl. poet. 32.
evidence.locator: Two canvases labeled fol. 030r and fol. 057r
evidence.summary: Proves that the published object has exactly two canvases and no target fol. 54v or 55r image; supplies exact canvas and Image API URLs.

evidence.citation: Audit of the cached complete Digital Bodleian two-canvas selection.
evidence.summary: ZIP integrity passed; inventory count equals manifest count; exact requests use 1800,; image 1 is fol. 30r at 1800 × 2734 and image 2 is fol. 57r at 1800 × 2751; visual inspection confirms both labels and zero target overlap.
States attribution and CC BY-NC reuse conditions for item images, distinguishes noncommercial academic publication from commercial use, and directs commercial users to seek permission.
Supplies the whole-object reproduction service and imaging@bodleian.ox.ac.uk contact route.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Remote access: Special Collections mediated copying.
evidence.summary: States that all users may request mediated copies, gives the current £1-per-scan non-Oxford reference-PDF rate, and explains service limitations and exceptions.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Copyright and reproducing images; Imaging Services Terms of Use and Image Order Form.
evidence.summary: Supports the distinction between private or noncommercial academic use and commercial or nonacademic publication, which requires separate permission; preserves current order and terms documents locally.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Special Collections manuscript requests.
evidence.summary: Supports registered-reader ordering and Weston Library consultation of the physical manuscript.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 1.0, records 5530 and 323, Zenodo record 14585961.
evidence.summary: Supports IMEV/NIMEV identifiers, titles, incipits, forms and subjects, the Rawlinson loci and Zupitza pages, five currently indexed Prouerbis witnesses, and the sole indexed Ten Commandments witness. It also exposes the 106-line description versus the 146-line Rawlinson component and Longleat inclusion that later scholarship disputes.

evidence.citation: Valerie J. Edden and Caroline Thompson, 'Middle English Verse Proverbs: The Problem of Classification,' Leeds Studies in English, new series 31 (2000): 173–203.
evidence.summary: Peer-reviewed support for treating proverb groups as fluid discourse colonies, identifying the organizer as compiler rather than assuming a single author, treating each compilation as a separate item, recognizing four substantially related Prouerbis versions with differing order and contents, and rejecting the Longleat text as unrelated to IMEV 3502.

open_questions.question: Can the Bodleian supply or authorize a complete color digital facsimile of the entire current physical MS. Rawl. poet. 32, and what exact storage, Git-publication, viewer, print-on-demand, and commercial permissions would govern it?
next_steps: Request the complete current object, including covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, every leaf, edges and detached matter where offered.
Map the work separately to fols. 54v–55r; do not request or retain only a target-leaf crop as the manuscript facsimile.
Retain the repository's quotation, delivery inventory, correspondence, and exact licence with any supplied files.

open_questions.question: When and where was the Rawlinson compilation assembled, and how should Zupitza's tentative Eastern Midlands authorial localization be reconciled with the current catalog's western Leicestershire or Warwickshire scribal-language statement?
next_steps: Keep the tentative authorial dialect claim, witness scribal dialect, and unknown compilation place separate.
Consult current linguistic and codicological scholarship before assigning a narrower work origin.
Inspect target folios in a complete facsimile for hand, ruling, ink, and boundary evidence.

open_questions.question: Why does DIMEV describe Prouerbis of Wysdom as 106 lines while the Rawlinson rubricated main component runs through line 146, and which of the later couplets should be represented as independently circulating indexed items?
next_steps: Compare DIMEV's record-level scope with its individual cross-referenced couplet records.
Preserve the 146-line Rawlinson manuscript component as the local cataloging boundary until a critical edition provides a better version model.
Do not explode every couplet into an independent work solely because parallels exist.

open_questions.question: Should Longleat MS 29 remain in the secure witness census for DIMEV 5530?
next_steps: Preserve DIMEV v1.0's five-witness census as a versioned dataset claim.
Preserve Edden and Thompson's reasoned rejection of the Longleat sequence as unrelated.
Consult the manuscript or a modern edition before choosing between the claims.
